1: 'I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys' — the *chavatzelet hasharon* is likely not the modern rose but a bulb flower: narcissus, crocus, or perhaps the autumn crocus that blooms on the coastal plain of Sharon. The Sharon (*hasharon*) was the fertile coastal plain between Jaffa and Mount Carmel, known for its abundant wildflowers. The self-description is modest — a wildflower, not a cultivated garden specimen — a contrast to the elaborate descriptions the beloved will apply to her.
2: 'Like a lily among thorns' — the beloved's response immediately elevates her self-deprecating comparison. The sharp contrast between the lily and the surrounding thorns makes her stand out as extraordinary against the ordinary. In ANE love poetry, such escalating comparisons were conventional: the woman compares herself modestly; the man responds by raising the comparison. The daughters (other young women) are the 'thorns' by contrast — not an insult but a poetic device to establish her unique beauty.
3: 'Like an apple tree among the trees of the forest' — the *tapuach* (possibly apricot or quince rather than modern apple; quince was the most fragrant) is distinguished from the wild forest trees by its fruit and its shade. Sitting in shade was a practical and precious comfort in the near east where heat was a real threat. The shade-giver (*tzel*) appears also in Psalms 121:5 (YHWH is your shade) and Isaiah 49:2 (hidden in the shadow of his hand). The image of rest under the beloved's shade is both sensory and protective.
4: 'He brought me to the banquet hall' — literally 'the house of wine' (*beit haiayin*), the dining room of the palace or estate. The banner (*degel*) was a military standard carried before troops to identify and rally them — each tribe of Israel had a banner (Num 2). The beloved's banner over her is 'love': he has publicly identified her as his own, placed her under his protection and claim. The honor-shame social structure of the ancient near east made this public claiming essential — she is under his banner, socially protected.
5: 'Strengthen me with raisins, revive me with apples' — *ashishot* (raisin cakes or pressed grape clusters) were a concentrated energy food and also used as offering gifts (Hosea 3:1 associates them with Baal worship, suggesting their festive associations). Lovesickness (*halat ahavah*, 'sick with love') was a recognized condition in ANE literature — Egyptian love poetry describes the physical symptoms of love's absence (pallor, loss of appetite, racing heart). The need for food-revival frames love as an overwhelming physical experience.
6: 'His left arm under my head, his right arm holds me close' — the protective embrace of the sleeping couple; the left arm as a pillow, the right arm as an encircling hold. This posture of mutual rest and protection recurs as a refrain in the Song (8:3 repeats it). The arm under the head was also a posture depicted in Egyptian love imagery.
7: 'I charge you by the gazelles and the does of the field: do not rouse or awaken love until it pleases' — a liturgical-style oath formula (*hishbati* = I adjure you) using wild animals as witnesses. The gazelle (*tzvi*) and doe (*ayalah*) were associated with swiftness, grace, and the wild freedom of nature; they invoke the untamed world as the context for this charge. The threefold refrain of this charge (Song of Solomon 2:7; Song of Solomon 3:5; Song of Solomon 8:4) functions as the Song's primary warning: love, once awakened, is powerful and must not be disturbed; its timing is not subject to human management.
8: 'Listen — my beloved! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains' — the lover's arrival is announced with the same urgency of a royal messenger. The leaping (*medaleig*) and bounding (*meqaphetz*) across mountains and hills echoes Psalms 18:33 (he makes my feet like deer's feet) and Habakkuk 3:19. The gazelle/stag comparison applied to the lover marks him as swift, sure-footed, and graceful — qualities valued in both warriors and noblemen in ANE culture.
9: 'Standing behind our wall, peering through the windows' — the architecture of separation: the beloved is outside, looking in through latticed windows (*charakkim*). Ancient near eastern houses were built with inner courtyards and latticed windows facing inward or outward; the lattice (*eshnav*) provided ventilation and partial visibility without full exposure. The peering beloved is caught between outside and inside, desiring entry but not yet admitted — the scene of longing through separation.
10: 'Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come with me' — the invitation formula (*qumi lakh*) is a summons to movement and departure. The double address 'my love' (*rayati*) and 'my beautiful one' (*yafati*) combines relational affection with aesthetic praise; both terms appear repeatedly throughout the Song as the beloved's primary titles. The call to 'come' (*lekhi lakh*) echoes the divine call to Abraham ('go forth' uses the same reflexive *lekh-lekha* construction, Genesis 12:1) — a summons that changes the course of a life.
11: 'For the winter is past; the rains are over and gone' — the Palestinian agricultural year divided into the dry season (summer, April-October) and the rainy season (winter, October-March). The end of the rains marked the beginning of the agricultural year's productive phase: plowing, planting, and the appearance of new growth. Spring was the season of Passover and the feast of firstfruits — associated with liberation (the Exodus) and new creation. The beloved's invitation to come is set in the context of renewal and release.
12: 'Flowers appear all across the land; the season of singing has arrived; the voice of the turtledove is heard' — the three signs of spring in Israel: wildflower bloom, the return of the turtledove (*tor*), and the season of song (*zmir*, also the word for pruning — a season both of pruning vines and of singing). The turtledove was a migratory bird (Jeremiah 8:7) whose spring arrival signaled the season's change; it was also the offering of the poor at the temple (Leviticus 5:7; Song of Solomon 12:8; Luke 2:24), giving it a cultic resonance.
13: 'The fig tree is ripening its early fruit; the blossoming vines spread their fragrance' — the fig (*teenah*) produced an early crop of small figs (*paggim*, green figs) before the main harvest; their ripening indicated late spring. The blossoming vineyard (*gefen*, vine; *semadar*, blossom) had a distinctive sweet fragrance as the flowers opened before setting fruit. The beloved's invitation to 'come' is framed by the agricultural signs of the land at its most fruitful moment — an invitation into abundance.
14: 'My dove, hidden in the clefts of the rock, in the sheltered crevice of the cliff' — rock clefts were the natural hiding places of doves in the ancient near east; the dove's safety was in its inaccessibility. The image of the beloved hidden and beautiful in the rock echoes Exodus 33:21-23 where YHWH places Moses in the cleft of the rock to shield him from the divine glory while allowing a partial view. The beloved, like Moses in the cleft, is both protected and desirous of fuller presence; the request is to see and hear her.
15: 'Catch the little foxes that ruin the vineyards — our vineyards are in bloom' — the foxes (*shualim*; likely jackals or young foxes) that damage vineyards were a real agricultural threat, noted in ANE agricultural texts. The vineyard 'in bloom' (*semadar*) is at its most vulnerable stage: the blossoms must set fruit before any damage ruins the crop. Interpretively, the little foxes may represent the small threats and distractions that endanger a relationship in its early, tender phase — the 'in bloom' stage of love that needs protection.
16: 'My beloved is mine and I am his; he grazes among the lilies' — the refrain of mutual possession (*dodi li va'ani lo*) is the Song's central declaration, restated three times in slightly varying forms (Song of Solomon 6:3; Song of Solomon 7:10). It reflects the covenant language of YHWH and Israel: 'I will be your God, and you will be my people' (Jeremiah 30:22; Ezekiel 36:28). The shepherd imagery ('grazes among the lilies') locates the beloved in the pastoral paradise — not among thorns (contrast Song of Solomon 2:2) but in the most beautiful pasture.
17: 'Until the day breaks and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved — be like a gazelle on the rugged mountains' — the 'mountains of separation' (*harei bater*; *beter* possibly means 'divided' or 'rugged') is an uncertain location; it may refer to specific hills or simply describe steep, divided terrain. The request is for the beloved to remain through the night and be present until dawn. The dawn (*shachar*) and the disappearing shadows frame a single night: the beloved is asked to stay from darkness until the first light — the most intimate request of the chapter.
